skill.name: azure-functions
skill.description: Expert patterns for Azure Functions development including isolated worker model, Durable Functions orchestration, cold start optimization, and production patterns. Covers .NET, Python, and Node.js
skill.summary: Production-ready patterns for serverless development on Azure Functions across .NET, Python, and Node.js runtimes.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged two false positives for weak cryptographic algorithms at lines 3 and 46. Manual review confirms these are documentation strings with no actual cryptographic code. The SKILL.md file contains only descriptive content about Azure Functions patterns - no executable code, network calls, file operations, or external commands. Safe for publication.

content.value_statement: Building serverless solutions on Azure often means grappling with cold starts and orchestration complexity. This skill delivers proven patterns for the isolated worker model, Durable Functions, and performance tuning.
actual_capabilities: Guide implementation of isolated worker model patterns for .NET Azure Functions
Provide Durable Functions orchestration templates for complex workflows
Optimize cold start performance with warmup triggers and configuration
Recommend language-specific best practices for .NET, Python, and Node.js
Identify and correct common anti-patterns in function design
limitations: Focuses on Azure Functions platform only, not other serverless providers
Requires Azure subscription for actual deployment and testing
Does not cover Azure Container Apps or other compute alternatives
Patterns may require adaptation for specific enterprise compliance requirements

use_cases.title: Build Event-Driven Serverless APIs
use_cases.description: Create scalable HTTP-triggered functions with proper connection pooling and async patterns for production workloads.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers building microservices on Azure

use_cases.title: Implement Complex Workflow Orchestration
use_cases.description: Design durable function chains for multi-step business processes with built-in retry and state management.

use_cases.title: Migrate Legacy Services to Serverless
use_cases.description: Transform existing in-process functions to isolated worker model with improved performance and dependency isolation.

prompt_templates.title: Setup Isolated Worker for .NET
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new Azure Functions project using the isolated worker model for .NET 8. Include an HTTP trigger endpoint that processes JSON payloads and returns validated responses. Add proper dependency injection setup and configuration binding.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new Azure Functions project with modern .NET

prompt_templates.title: Design Durable Functions Orchestration
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Durable Functions orchestration that processes orders through multiple stages: validation, payment processing, inventory check, and shipping notification. Include error handling with retry policies and human approval gates for high-value orders.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Cold Start Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my Azure Functions configuration for cold start issues. The function runs Python with a large ML model. Recommend specific settings for premium plan, instance warming, and package optimization to achieve sub-second response times.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate In-Process to Isolated Worker
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this existing in-process Azure Function to the isolated worker model. Update the function signature, binding attributes, and dependency injection pattern. Highlight any breaking changes I need to address.

output_examples.input: Request guidance on Azure Functions connection management
output_examples.output: Recommended approach: Use IHttpClientFactory for HTTP connections. This provides connection pooling, DNS refresh, and prevents socket exhaustion. Never instantiate HttpClient directly in function code.

output_examples.input: Ask about Durable Functions pattern for fan-out processing
output_examples.output: Use the Fan-out/Fan-in pattern: The orchestrator calls multiple activity functions in parallel using Task.WhenAll, then aggregates results. Ideal for batch processing, data aggregation, or parallel API calls.

output_examples.input: Need help with Python Azure Functions v2 model
output_examples.output: The v2 model uses decorators like @app.function_name and @app.route. Key benefits: IntelliSense support, simplified binding syntax, and unified programming model. Import azure.functions module and define routes with HTTP methods.

best_practices: Always use the isolated worker model for new .NET projects to benefit from process isolation and side-by-side versioning
Implement async/await patterns throughout to prevent thread pool starvation and improve throughput
Reuse clients and connections via dependency injection or static instances to avoid connection exhaustion
anti_patterns: Blocking async calls with .Result or .Wait() which causes deadlocks and thread pool exhaustion
Creating new HttpClient instances per request instead of using IHttpClientFactory for connection pooling
Using the legacy in-process model for new projects when isolated worker provides better isolation and compatibility

faq.question: What is the difference between in-process and isolated worker models?
faq.answer: In-process runs inside the Azure Functions host process, while isolated worker runs in a separate process. Isolated worker supports side-by-side .NET versions, better dependency isolation, and is the recommended model for new projects.

faq.question: How do I reduce cold start times for my Azure Functions?
faq.answer: Use Premium plan or dedicated App Service plan, implement warmup triggers, minimize package dependencies, enable ReadyToRun compilation for .NET, and consider using smaller deployment packages with external dependencies.

faq.question: When should I use Durable Functions versus regular functions?
faq.answer: Use Durable Functions when you need stateful orchestration, complex workflows with multiple steps, fan-out/fan-in patterns, or long-running processes with human interaction. Use regular functions for simple stateless operations.

faq.question: What are the timeout limits for Azure Functions?
faq.answer: Consumption plan has a maximum timeout of 5-10 minutes depending on configuration. Premium and dedicated plans support up to 30 minutes. For longer processes, use Durable Functions with built-in checkpointing.

faq.question: How do I handle secrets and configuration in Azure Functions?
faq.answer: Use Azure Key Vault references in Application Settings, Managed Identity for authentication, and avoid hardcoding secrets. Access configuration via environment variables or IConfiguration in .NET isolated worker.

faq.question: Can I use Azure Functions with containers?
faq.answer: Yes, Azure Functions supports containerized deployment on Azure Container Instances, Azure Kubernetes Service, or Azure Container Apps. This provides more control over the runtime environment and dependencies.
